skill.name: ace-step
skill.description: Generate, inpaint, and outpaint music with ACE Step on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. ACE Step is StepFun-AI's open-weights music foundation model — tag-driven composition (genre, mood, instruments), multilingual lyrics with section markers, 5 s to 4 min stereo output, $0.0002–0.0003 per second (≈ 27× cheaper than ElevenLabs Music). Four endpoints: ACE Step text-to-audio (the default), ACE Step 1.5 text-to-audio (50+ language lyrics, refined structured-lyric handling), ACE Step audio-inpaint (regenerate a time range inside an existing track), ACE Step audio-outpaint (extend an existing track before or after). Triggers on "ace step", "ace-step", "acestep", "ACE music", "open music model", "cheap AI music", "inpaint audio", "audio inpaint", "extend music", "audio outpaint", "lengthen track", "music with tags", or any explicit ask to generate or edit music with ACE Step.
skill.summary: Generate, edit, and extend music using the ACE Step open-weights model via the RunComfy CLI with tag-driven composition and multilingual vocals.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: All 146 static analysis findings are false positives. The 114 external_commands detections are Ruby shell backtick patterns inside markdown code blocks showing runcomfy CLI usage examples, not active code. The skill legitimately invokes the runcomfy CLI (declared via allowed-tools: Bash(runcomfy *)) with JSON arguments that are not shell-expanded, which is a safe pattern. The 21 network findings are documentation links to runcomfy.com and related service URLs. The filesystem and weak crypto findings are markdown text, not code. No malicious intent, no prompt injection, and no dangerous data exfiltration detected.

medium_findings.title: External command execution in documentation code blocks
medium_findings.description: 114 instances of Ruby shell backtick patterns were detected across the SKILL.md file. All instances are within markdown fenced code blocks showing runcomfy CLI usage examples. The skill is legitimately designed to invoke the runcomfy CLI (declared in allowed-tools: Bash(runcomfy *) with scoped subcommand access). Arguments are passed as JSON via --input flag and are not shell-expanded, which prevents command injection. This is a false positive at the pattern level since the code is in documentation blocks, but the external command pattern is expected and safe for this skill type.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All 114 detections are within markdown code blocks, not active code. The allowed-tools declaration scopes execution to runcomfy only. No command injection vector exists since arguments are JSON-encoded and not shell-expanded.

medium_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm detection is a false positive
medium_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithms at lines 5, 111, 132, 248, and 302. These lines contain YAML frontmatter text, JSON input examples, prompting tips, and an exit codes table. None of these lines contain any cryptographic algorithm references. The scanner likely matched a false pattern from surrounding content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed all five flagged lines - none contain cryptographic references. Line 5 is YAML description, line 111 is JSON input, line 132 is prompting tips, line 248 is outpainting tips, line 302 is an exit codes table header.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in documentation links
low_findings.description: 21 hardcoded URLs were detected. All are legitimate service URLs pointing to runcomfy.com, docs.runcomfy.com, and skills.sh domains. These are documentation links for the RunComfy platform that the skill wraps. The URLs are primarily reference links in markdown and UTM-tracked links to the service homepage and model catalog pages.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to the RunComfy platform which the skill explicitly wraps. No suspicious domains, no data exfiltration endpoints, no pastebin or raw data hosting URLs detected.

low_findings.title: Hidden file reference in documentation
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged line 322 for hidden file access (~/.config/runcomfy/token.json). This is part of the Security & Privacy section documenting where the runcomfy CLI stores authentication tokens. The text explains token storage with mode 0600 permissions. No actual code reads or writes this file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reference is documentation explaining CLI behavior. The skill does not contain code to read or write the token file. The Security & Privacy section explicitly warns against echoing or logging tokens.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance patterns in documentation
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged lines 70, 75, 80, and 85 for system reconnaissance. These are 'Pick for' and 'Avoid for' bullet points in endpoint selection guidance, describing legitimate use cases for each ACE Step endpoint. No system enumeration or reconnaissance activity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Flagged lines are plain markdown text providing usage guidance for ACE Step endpoints. No commands, system calls, or reconnaissance patterns present.

content.user_title: Generate and edit music with ACE Step AI on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Creating custom music for projects usually requires expensive subscriptions or audio expertise. This skill lets you generate, edit, and extend original music using the ACE Step open-weights model through simple text prompts and tags.
actual_capabilities: Generate original music from text prompts and tag descriptions (genre, mood, instruments, BPM)
Produce vocal songs with structured lyrics using section markers like Verse, Chorus, and Bridge
Regenerate specific time ranges inside existing audio tracks (audio inpainting)
Extend existing tracks by adding intro before or outro after (audio outpainting)
Support 50+ languages for multilingual vocal lyrics with ACE Step 1.5
Generate stereo audio from 5 seconds to 4 minutes per call at low cost
limitations: Maximum 4 minutes of audio per generation call (240 seconds)
Audio inpainting and outpainting require an existing audio file accessible via HTTPS URL
Does not support mask-based inpainting or instrument separation
Polished commercial vocal tracks may require premium alternatives for best quality

use_cases.title: Background music for videos and podcasts
use_cases.description: Content creators can generate custom background music by describing the desired mood, genre, and instruments. Generate multiple variations quickly at low cost and pick the best fit.

use_cases.title: Game audio loops and soundtracks
use_cases.description: Game developers can produce loopable music beds and themed tracks using tag-driven prompts. Generate seamless loop variations for different game scenes and levels.

use_cases.title: Song prototyping and arrangement
use_cases.description: Musicians and producers can draft song ideas with full vocal structure, then use inpainting to replace sections or outpainting to extend arrangements without re-rendering the whole track.

prompt_templates.title: Simple instrumental generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a lo-fi hip-hop instrumental with a mellow mood, vinyl crackle, rhodes piano, and soft drums at 75 BPM. Make it 90 seconds long with no vocals.
prompt_templates.scenario: A beginner wants to create background music without complex parameters.

prompt_templates.title: Vocal song with structured lyrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an indie pop song with anthemic electric guitar and driving drums at 120 BPM. Use these lyrics with sections: Verse about morning, Chorus about rising up, Bridge with piano breakdown, and Outro with full band fade.
prompt_templates.scenario: A songwriter wants to hear a full vocal arrangement with section markers.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a section inside an existing track
prompt_templates.prompt: Replace the section from 20 to 40 seconds in this audio file with a piano-only breakdown. Match the indie pop style of the original but make it soft with no drums.
prompt_templates.scenario: A producer recorded a song but wants to replace the bridge with a different arrangement.

prompt_templates.title: Extend a short track into a full arrangement
prompt_templates.prompt: Take this 30-second instrumental hook and extend it into a 2-minute track. Add a 30-second intro that builds up and a 60-second outro with a fade. Keep the indie pop electric guitar style throughout.
prompt_templates.scenario: A composer has a short hook and needs to develop it into a complete song structure.

output_examples.input: Generate an ambient electronic pad with soft reverb, 90 BPM, 60 seconds, no vocals.
output_examples.output: A 60-second stereo WAV file containing a warm ambient pad with gradual filter sweeps and soft reverb tails. The track has a calm, meditative atmosphere suitable for background scoring.

output_examples.input: Replace the section from 15 seconds to the end of the track with a soft ambient pad fade-out. The original is an indie pop song.
output_examples.output: The original track's last 15 seconds are replaced with a new ambient pad segment that gradually fades to silence. The transition blends smoothly with the preceding section.

best_practices: Use specific comma-separated tags for genre, mood, instruments, and BPM instead of vague descriptions for better output quality
Include BPM in tags when tempo consistency matters, as ACE Step respects tempo language in prompts
Use the audio-inpaint endpoint for targeted fixes rather than re-generating entire tracks from scratch
anti_patterns: Do not paste untrusted audio URLs into inpaint or outpaint prompts as they may contain embedded instructions that influence generation
Do not use vague single-word tags like 'chill music' - they produce inconsistent results compared to detailed tag lists
Do not exceed the 4-minute total limit when combining original track length with outpainting extensions

faq.question: What is ACE Step?
faq.answer: ACE Step is an open-weights music foundation model by StepFun-AI. It generates tag-driven music with optional lyrics, audio inpainting, and outpainting capabilities.

faq.question: How much does ACE Step cost?
faq.answer: ACE Step costs $0.0002 per second of audio generated. ACE Step 1.5 costs $0.0003 per second. A 60-second track costs about $0.012 to $0.018.

faq.question: Can ACE Step generate songs with vocals?
faq.answer: Yes. You can provide lyrics with section markers like Verse, Chorus, and Bridge. ACE Step 1.5 supports vocals in 50+ languages.

faq.question: How do I extend existing music with outpainting?
faq.answer: Use the audio-outpaint endpoint. Provide an HTTPS URL to your audio file and set extend_before_duration for an intro or extend_after_duration for an outro.

faq.question: What is the difference between ACE Step and ACE Step 1.5?
faq.answer: ACE Step 1.5 offers better multilingual vocal support and refined structured-lyric handling. It costs slightly more ($0.0003/s vs $0.0002/s) but produces better quality vocal tracks.

faq.question: Is it safe to use this skill in automated environments?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill uses the runcomfy CLI with JSON arguments that are not shell-expanded. Network traffic goes only to runcomfy.net and runcomfy.com. Tokens can be set via the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable.
